content.problemStatement:
Write a C program that generates and prints the fibonacci(n) using a recurssion  function. The function should take an integer n as an argument and returns the Fibonacci to the nth term.

**Note:** The **Fibonacci sequence** F0,F1,…FnF_0, F_1, \ldots F_nF0​,F1​,…Fn​ is a special infinite sequence of non-negative integers, where F0=0F_0 = 0F0​=0, F1=1F_1 = 1F1​=1 and for each integer n≥2n \ge 2n≥2, Fn=Fn−1+Fn−2F_n = F_{n-1} + F_{n-2}Fn​=Fn−1​+Fn−2​.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N denoting the term of the Fibonacci sequence to compute.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the Nth Fibonacci number on its own line.

sampleTestCases.explanation: There are three test cases. N=6 → Fibonacci(6)=8. N=9 → Fibonacci(9)=34. N=1 → Fibonacci(1)=1.
Two test cases. N=2 → Fibonacci(2)=1. N=3 → Fibonacci(3)=2.
Single test case with the maximum allowed N. Fibonacci(30)=832040.

solution.approach: The Fibonacci sequence can be defined recursively: F(0)=0, F(1)=1 and for n>=2, F(n)=F(n-1)+F(n-2). Because N is at most 30, a plain recursive implementation is fast enough, but we store already computed values in a memoisation array of size 31, turning the recursion into O(N) time per test case. Each test case is independent, so the memo array is re‑initialised for every call.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (with memoisation), O(T·N) total. Without memoisation it would be O(2^N) but still acceptable for N≤30.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) recursion stack plus O(N) memo array, i.e., O(N) auxiliary space.
